page: Microsoft Azure EC4as_v6 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine belonging to the ECasv6 server family. Powered by an AMD processor, this instance features 4 dedicated vCPUs and 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. The underlying virtualization is managed via the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor.

This server configuration does not include any local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met using external volumes. It is, however, designated as Premium Storage capable, allowing the attachment of high-performance remote disks. There are zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses bundled with this instance. No graphics processing units are present, making it unsuitable for hardware-accelerated rendering or parallel computing tasks.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the EC4as_v6 offers a high memory density relative to its compute allocation, positioning it as a resource-efficient option for memory-bound workloads that do not require massive CPU scaling. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent processing performance without the resource contention typical of shared-core environments. This profile is appropriate for relational databases, in-memory caching layers, and enterprise applications requiring a moderate compute footprint paired with a larger memory capacity.
description: Microsoft Azure EC4as_v6 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine from the ECasv6 family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It is equipped with an AMD processor providing 4 dedicated vCPUs and 32.0 GB of system memory, establishing a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. This instance does not feature any local storage, though it is Premium Storage capable, enabling the attachment of high-performance remote storage volumes. No GPUs are included in this configuration, and it comes with zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Qualitatively, the EC4as_v6 provides a cost-effective balance for memory-intensive workloads that require dedicated compute resources without the overhead of massive CPU scaling. It is suited for workloads such as medium-sized databases, in-memory caching, and enterprise applications that demand a high memory-to-CPU ratio.
